epigraph.text: The heavens declare the glory of God, and the sky above proclaims his handiwork.

content.text:
The relationship between angles and arcs in a circle is among the most 
elegant in all of geometry. When we place an angle's vertex at different 
positions relative to a circle—at the center, on the circle, or outside 
the circle—we discover remarkable patterns that connect the angle to 
the arc it "sees." These relationships have practical applications in 
astronomy, navigation, and engineering.
Arc Measure
Before studying angles in circles, we need a way to measure arcs.

content.title: Arc Measure
content.content:
The **measure of an arc** is defined as follows:

- The measure of a **minor arc** equals the measure of 
 its central angle.

- The measure of a **major arc** equals $360°$ minus the 
 measure of the corresponding minor arc.

- The measure of a **semicircle** is $180°$.

content.text:
We write arc measure using the notation $m\widearc{AB}$ to denote "the 
measure of arc $AB$." Note that arc measure is in degrees, not in 
units of length. (Arc *length* is a different concept, which 
we will study later.)
Central Angles

content.title: Central Angle
content.content:
A **central angle** of a circle is an angle 
whose vertex is at the center of the circle.

content.title: Central Angle--Arc Relationship
content.content:
The measure of a central angle equals the measure of its intercepted arc:
\[
 m\angle AOB = m\widearc{AB}
\]
where $O$ is the center of the circle.
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
This relationship is essentially definitional—we *define* the 
measure of an arc to be the measure of its central angle. This provides 
a natural way to measure portions of a circle.

content.title: Central angles and arcs
content.solution:
1. The measure of the minor arc equals the central angle:
 \[m\widearc{AB} = m\angle AOB = 70°\]

2. The major arc is the remainder of the circle:
 \[m\widearc{ADB} = 360° - m\widearc{AB} = 360° - 70° = 290°\]

content.text: Inscribed Angles

content.title: Inscribed Angle
content.content:
An **inscribed angle** is an angle whose 
vertex lies on the circle and whose sides are chords of the circle.

content.title: Intercepted Arc
content.content:
The arc that lies in the interior of an inscribed angle (between the 
two sides) is called the **intercepted arc**.

content.text:
The following theorem reveals a beautiful relationship between inscribed 
angles and their intercepted arcs.

content.title: Inscribed Angle Theorem
content.content:
The measure of an inscribed angle is half the measure of its 
intercepted arc:
\[
 m\angle ABC = \frac{1}{2} m\widearc{AC}
\]
where $B$ is on the circle and $A$, $C$ are the endpoints of the 
intercepted arc.
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
*Proof sketch:* We consider three cases depending on the 
position of the center $O$ relative to the inscribed angle.

**Case 1:** One side of the angle passes through the center. 
Let $\overline{BO}$ be a diameter. Since $\triangle OBC$ is isosceles 
(radii $OB = OC$), we have $\angle OBC = \angle OCB$. The central 
angle $\angle AOC$ is an exterior angle of $\triangle OBC$, so 
$\angle AOC = 2\angle OBC = 2\angle ABC$. Thus 
$m\angle ABC = \frac{1}{2}m\widearc{AC}$.

**Cases 2 and 3:** When the center is inside or outside the 
inscribed angle, we draw a diameter through $B$ and apply Case 1 
to both parts, then add or subtract.

content.title: Using the Inscribed Angle Theorem
content.solution:
1. The inscribed angle is half the intercepted arc:
 \[x = \frac{1}{2}(100) = 50°\]

2. The inscribed angle is half the intercepted arc, so:
 \[35 = \frac{1}{2}x \implies x = 70°\]

content.text: Corollaries of the Inscribed Angle Theorem
The Inscribed Angle Theorem leads to several important corollaries.

content.title: Corollary 1: Angles Inscribed in the Same Arc
content.content:
If two inscribed angles intercept the same arc, then the angles 
are congruent.
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
*Proof:* Both angles have the same intercepted arc $\widearc{AC}$, 
so by the Inscribed Angle Theorem, both equal $\frac{1}{2}m\widearc{AC}$.

content.title: Corollary 2: Angle Inscribed in a Semicircle
content.content: An angle inscribed in a semicircle is a right angle.
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
*Proof:* The inscribed angle intercepts a semicircle, which 
has measure $180°$. By the Inscribed Angle Theorem:
\[m\angle ACB = \frac{1}{2}(180°) = 90°\]

This beautiful theorem, sometimes called **Thales' Theorem**%
, is attributed to the ancient Greek philosopher 
Thales of Miletus (c. 624–546 BCE).

content.title: Using Thales' Theorem
content.solution:
By Thales' Theorem, $\angle ACB = 90°$ since it is inscribed in a 
semicircle. Thus $\triangle ACB$ is a right triangle with legs 
$AC = 5$ and $BC = 12$. By the Pythagorean Theorem:
$$
 AB^2 &= AC^2 + BC^2 \\
 AB^2 &= 5^2 + 12^2 = 25 + 144 = 169 \\
 AB &= 13
$$

content.title: Corollary 3: Inscribed Quadrilateral
content.content:
The opposite angles of a quadrilateral inscribed in a circle are 
supplementary (sum to $180°$).
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
*Proof:* Angle $A$ intercepts arc $BCD$, and angle $C$ intercepts 
arc $DAB$. Together, these arcs form the entire circle ($360°$). Thus:

$$m\angle A + m\angle C &= \frac{1}{2}m\widearc{BCD} + \frac{1}{2}m\widearc{DAB} \\
    &= \frac{1}{2}(m\widearc{BCD} + m\widearc{DAB}) \\
    &= \frac{1}{2}(360°) = 180°$$
A similar argument shows $\angle B + \angle D = 180°$.

content.title: Inscribed quadrilateral
content.problem:
Quadrilateral $ABCD$ is inscribed in a circle. If $m\angle A = 85°$ 
and $m\angle B = 110°$, find $m\angle C$ and $m\angle D$.
content.solution:
By Corollary 3, opposite angles are supplementary:
$$
 m\angle C &= 180° - m\angle A = 180° - 85° = 95° \\
 m\angle D &= 180° - m\angle B = 180° - 110° = 70°
$$

We can verify: $85° + 110° + 95° + 70° = 360°$

content.text: Comparing Central and Inscribed Angles

A key insight is that a central angle and an inscribed angle that 
intercept the same arc are related by a factor of 2.

content.title: Central vs.\ inscribed
content.solution:
The central angle equals the arc measure: $m\widearc{AC} = 84°$.

The inscribed angle is half the arc:
\[m\angle ABC = \frac{1}{2}(84°) = 42°\]

content.text: Exercises

marginNotes.text:
He determines the number of the stars; 
he gives to all of them their names.

When he established the heavens, I was 
there; when he drew a circle on the face of the deep.

Who has measured the waters in the 
hollow of his hand and marked off the heavens with a span?
